title: Dude Wheres My Ranch

jokes.text: Makes me wish I hadn't released the hounds. [ALL SCREAMING AND DOGS BARKING]
jokes.setup: Mr. Burns is moved by the Christmas caroling
jokes.punchline: His sentimentality immediately causes regret about unleashing attack dogs on carolers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - only he would have this exact reaction to Christmas sentiment. Dark humor executed with perfect timing and character consistency.

jokes.text: Should I call them off, sir? - No, no. It's their Christmas too.
jokes.setup: Smithers offers to stop the dog attack
jokes.punchline: Burns justifies continued violence as holiday spirit for the dogs
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the hounds joke, maintaining Burns' twisted logic about holiday generosity.

jokes.text: That's even worse than 'I'm Dreaming of a Whitefish Christmas.' For this, I tied my bathrobe?
jokes.setup: The family sings an awkward politically correct Christmas song
jokes.punchline: Krusty references an even worse Jewish Christmas parody and regrets getting dressed
jokes.explanation: Decent Jewish character humor with the whitefish pun, though fairly predictable.

jokes.text: They belong to everyone, like grapes at the grocery store.
jokes.setup: Homer argues nobody owns Christmas carols
jokes.punchline: His comparison reveals his casual theft mentality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - reveals his character through seemingly innocent comparison. The grape theft implication is both unexpected and perfectly in-character.

jokes.text: Those suck. They're worse than nothing.
jokes.setup: Lawyer suggests public domain Christmas carols
jokes.punchline: Homer's brutal dismissal of classic carols
jokes.explanation: Homer's blunt cultural philistinism, though the line itself isn't particularly memorable.

jokes.text: Don't use A-flat or G-natural. Those are owned by Disney. - Oh. - That's A-flat. - Oh! - That's better.
jokes.setup: Lawyer warns about Disney owning musical notes
jokes.punchline: Homer accidentally plays copyrighted notes and gets corrected
jokes.explanation: Creative absurdist take on Disney's copyright aggression, with good physical comedy timing.

jokes.text: If I can make my own clothes, I can do anything.
jokes.setup: Homer declares he'll write a Christmas carol
jokes.punchline: His confidence is based on his terrible homemade clothing
jokes.explanation: Homer's misplaced confidence, enhanced by visual implication of his awful homemade clothes.

jokes.text: Well, the Bible says it's fun to do stuff together.
jokes.setup: Ned tries to join Homer's songwriting
jokes.punchline: His vague, wholesome Biblical misinterpretation
jokes.explanation: Typical Ned behavior - awkward religious references that don't quite fit the situation.

jokes.text: Flanders, I am a patient, reasonable, handsome man.
jokes.setup: Homer is clearly frustrated with Ned
jokes.punchline: His self-description contrasts with his obvious irritation
jokes.explanation: Homer's delusional self-assessment while clearly being the opposite, especially including 'handsome.'

jokes.text: It's a popsicle in my favorite flavor, plain.
jokes.setup: Homer appears to be eating something
jokes.punchline: His favorite flavor is the absence of flavor
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical character joke - Homer's preference for 'plain' flavor is both absurd and perfectly fitting.

jokes.text: Everybody in the U.S.A. Hates their stupid neighbor / He's Flanders And he's really, really lame
jokes.setup: Homer transforms his frustration into a song
jokes.punchline: The petty, personal attack disguised as universal sentiment
jokes.explanation: The birth of the episode's central running gag. Great character work - Homer's petty grievance becomes a universal anthem.

jokes.text: And I used to wrestle under the name El Diablo. - I thought that was Philip Glass. - Yeah, he wishes.
jokes.setup: David Byrne lists his credentials
jokes.punchline: Confusion between avant-garde composers and lucha libre
jokes.explanation: Clever intellectual humor mixing high culture figures with wrestling personas.

jokes.text: Hey, mister, if you like my song so much, would you like to buy a tape? It's already rewound.
jokes.setup: David Byrne compliments Homer's song
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately tries to sell him a tape with the 'rewound' selling point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer entrepreneurial instincts - the 'already rewound' detail shows his amateur mindset.

jokes.text: LENNY & CARL: D-E-R HOMER: His wife is dead [WOMAN SCREAMS]
jokes.setup: The song continues with spelling out Flanders
jokes.punchline: Homer's tactless reference to Maude's death gets a horrified reaction
jokes.explanation: Dark callback humor - Homer's complete lack of tact about a tragic event gets appropriate shocked reaction.

jokes.text: - That's us. - Hurray!
jokes.setup: Song mentions Ned's 'creepy little offspring Rod and Todd'
jokes.punchline: The kids cheerfully identify themselves in the insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders family innocence - the kids are happy to be mentioned even in an insulting song.

jokes.text: Another cover? My God, let it die.
jokes.setup: The song has become oversaturated with covers
jokes.punchline: Lisa's exasperation mirrors audience fatigue with overplayed hits
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about overexposed pop culture phenomena, with Lisa as the voice of reason.

jokes.text: Now I know how God feels.
jokes.setup: Homer regrets creating his song
jokes.punchline: He compares himself to God regretting creation
jokes.explanation: Homer's grandiose comparison of his minor creative regret to divine disappointment with humanity.

jokes.text: What? It said 'take one.'
jokes.setup: Marge criticizes Homer for taking something from a motel
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation of promotional materials
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer literalism joke - predictable but in character.

jokes.text: 'The Denzel Washington Monument'
jokes.setup: Reading tourist attractions from a brochure
jokes.punchline: Monument named after actor instead of George Washington
jokes.explanation: Simple name substitution joke, moderately clever wordplay

jokes.text: Or we could go to Walter Gropius' Bauhaus Village. - And fight the crowds? Forget it.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggests a highbrow architectural destination
jokes.punchline: Homer assumes a Bauhaus exhibit would be crowded
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Lisa's intellectualism and Homer's misunderstanding of niche appeal.

jokes.text: Watch it, Mr. Byrne. You'll slip on the simonize. - Simonize? [YELLS]
jokes.setup: Moe warns David Byrne about slippery floor wax
jokes.punchline: Byrne's confusion about 'simonize' causes him to slip
jokes.explanation: Pun on car wax brand name creates physical comedy setup.

jokes.text: Wasn't that the hospital? - Uh, you ever see the movie Misery? - Actually, no. - Then this'll all be new to you.
jokes.setup: Moe drives past the hospital while taking injured Byrne there
jokes.punchline: Moe's ominous reference to Misery implies kidnapping intentions
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect Moe characterization - the Misery reference is both threatening and perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Son, the only Internet connection we got is a dedicated DSL port in the business center. Or you could patch in through the smart fridge in your cabin.
jokes.setup: Ranch claims to be rustic without Internet
jokes.punchline: They actually have multiple high-tech Internet options
jokes.explanation: Good satire of fake 'rustic' experiences that maintain modern conveniences.

jokes.text: Hmm, if said fridge is smart, it will be filled with Hot Pockets and bologna by the time I get there.
jokes.setup: Discussion of smart fridge technology
jokes.punchline: Bart's definition of 'smart' is purely food-focused
jokes.explanation: Good Bart logic - his practical interpretation of AI technology in terms of junk food.

jokes.text: On this battleground in 1881, 56 Indians lost their lives and four brave Americans lost their hats. [WHIMPERING] Those poor hats.
jokes.setup: Historical marker describes a battle with massive Native American casualties
jokes.punchline: Only mentions American hat losses, Lisa mourns the hats
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of sanitized historical narratives combined with Lisa's hilariously misplaced priorities.

jokes.text: This whole ranch is appalling. It's built on a legacy of cruelty to animals and oppression of indigenous people. - Just our way of making y'all feel welcome.
jokes.setup: Lisa criticizes the ranch's problematic history
jokes.punchline: Ranch owner treats historical atrocities as hospitality
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Lisa's activism and the ranch owner's cheerful obliviousness.

jokes.text: I'll show you how they get on a horse where I come from. - Daddy, I need upsies. - Coming, sweetie.
jokes.setup: Homer boasts about mounting horses
jokes.punchline: He needs help lifting his baby daughter
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy undermining Homer's bravado - his weakness revealed by needing help with basic parenting.

jokes.text: Pleased to meet you. Have a tooth.
jokes.setup: Cookie introduces himself to Lisa
jokes.punchline: He offers her one of his teeth
jokes.explanation: Absurd character introduction - economical way to establish Cookie's grotesque nature.

jokes.text: Hey, Crabby Hayes, get with the program.
jokes.setup: Lisa is complaining about ranch activities
jokes.punchline: Bart's reference to Western movie sidekick Gabby Hayes
jokes.explanation: Decent cultural reference joke, though somewhat dated and niche.

jokes.text: Mama rattlesnake would be mighty sore if you scrambled her young'un.
jokes.setup: Luke stops Lisa from disturbing a rattlesnake
jokes.punchline: Gentle pun on 'scrambled' eggs with snake protection
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing Luke's animal knowledge and gentle humor.

jokes.text: My precious.
jokes.setup: Cleanie collects garbage from dinner
jokes.punchline: Reference to Gollum from Lord of the Rings
jokes.explanation: Lazy pop culture reference - obvious comparison without adding anything new.

jokes.text: He unified the nations of the western plains. Five dollars or three for 12.
jokes.setup: Describes ancestor's noble historical role
jokes.punchline: Immediately shifts to tacky tourist pricing
jokes.explanation: Sharp juxtaposition between dignity and commercialism - effective satire of tourist trap mentality.

jokes.text: Can you shoot one where I'm Rambo and my wife is Betty Boop? - You mean package B.
jokes.setup: Tourist requests specific photo setup
jokes.punchline: It's a pre-planned tourist package option
jokes.explanation: Good satire of pre-packaged tourist experiences - the specificity makes it believable.

jokes.text: Then the river was dammed to make that lake by our ancient enemy, the beaver.
jokes.setup: Explains loss of tribal land
jokes.punchline: Blames beavers instead of government/developers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist twist on typical land displacement narrative - beavers as ancient enemies is perfectly ridiculous.

jokes.text: Unfortunately, the beaver is also our god. In retrospect, it was a poor choice.
jokes.setup: Establishes beaver as enemy
jokes.punchline: Reveals they worship their enemy, with understated regret
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist joke with perfect deadpan delivery. The 'poor choice' understatement makes it brilliant.

jokes.text: Sure. And when we do, your breakfast will be comped. - How many decks will your blackjack dealers use? - Eight. - Three. - Four. - Deal.
jokes.setup: Discussion of building a casino
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately negotiates casino terms like a gambling expert
jokes.explanation: Good Homer characterization - his gambling knowledge surfaces immediately when relevant.

jokes.text: I didn't gasp like that until I was 18.
jokes.setup: Lisa gasps dramatically at Luke
jokes.punchline: Marge's inappropriate overshare about her own romantic development
jokes.explanation: Marge's TMI moment - inappropriate parental observation delivered with perfect timing.

jokes.text: No! I wanted to die choking on food.
jokes.setup: Homer is about to be attacked by beavers
jokes.punchline: His specific preference for death by food
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - even facing death, he maintains his food-obsessed priorities.

jokes.text: Stop shoving. The little one isn't getting any.
jokes.setup: Homer is being attacked by beavers
jokes.punchline: He's concerned about fair beaver distribution during his mauling
jokes.explanation: Homer's bizarre fairness concerns during physical attack - absurdist character comedy.

jokes.text: Free range my ass. Tonight you're gonna be swimming next to a matzo ball.
jokes.setup: Luke shows Lisa the supposedly humane ranch
jokes.punchline: He reveals the chickens will be killed for soup
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of the 'gentle cowboy' character - reveals the reality beneath the facade.

jokes.text: Oh, it looks like a happy sheep. - Oh. - Oh, damn it.
jokes.setup: Looking at clouds for innocent shapes
jokes.punchline: The cloud apparently forms something inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Standard visual gag setup - clouds forming inappropriate shapes is a common trope.

jokes.text: Don't rightly know. I just soaked up the lingo like a biscuit in a bucket full of gopher gravy.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks why Marge is talking like a cowboy
jokes.punchline: Marge's elaborate Western metaphor for cultural absorption
jokes.explanation: Good Marge character moment - her overly elaborate explanation while caught in cowboy speak.

jokes.text: But when he's 23, I'll be 18.
jokes.setup: Marge points out Luke is 13 and Lisa is 8
jokes.punchline: Lisa's innocent calculation of future age compatibility
jokes.explanation: Good child innocence humor - Lisa's logical but naive romantic planning.

jokes.text: I didn't marry the first boy I... Well, I did, but you shouldn't.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to give Lisa dating advice
jokes.punchline: She realizes her advice contradicts her own life story
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-aware character moment - Marge catches herself mid-hypocrisy with perfect timing.

jokes.text: It's beef hormones and the fluorescent lights. What are you gonna do?
jokes.setup: Marge worries about kids growing up too fast
jokes.punchline: Homer's bizarre pseudo-scientific explanation with resignation
jokes.explanation: Good Homer pseudo-intellectualism - confidently wrong explanations delivered with authority.

jokes.text: [BRITNEY SPEARS' 'OOPS!... I DID IT AGAIN' PLAYING] Oops!... I did it again I played with your heart Got lost in the game
jokes.setup: Discussion about kids growing up too fast
jokes.punchline: Baby Maggie is listening to Britney Spears, appearing mature
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing baby influenced by pop culture reinforcing the theme

jokes.text: A dance? A dance? [LAUGHING AND WHOOPING]
jokes.setup: Someone mentions there's a dance tonight
jokes.punchline: Cookie's extreme excitement about dancing
jokes.explanation: Character moment establishing Cookie's enthusiastic nature about social events.

jokes.text: Whew. I'm pooped. I'm going to bed. - Bed? [WHOOPS]
jokes.setup: Cookie says he's tired and going to bed
jokes.punchline: Others react as if 'bed' is also exciting
jokes.explanation: Extension of Cookie's over-enthusiasm - even mundane activities get excited reactions.

jokes.text: Love you too, Clara.
jokes.setup: Lisa thinks Luke is interested in her romantically
jokes.punchline: Luke mentions loving Clara, creating jealousy
jokes.explanation: Setup for the Clara misdirection plot - creates romantic triangle tension.

jokes.text: But a quicker way to the ranch is that shortcut. Thanks, darling. See you at the dance.
jokes.setup: Clara asks for directions to the ranch
jokes.punchline: Lisa deliberately gives wrong directions to eliminate romantic rival
jokes.explanation: Lisa's uncharacteristic scheming - shows how crushes can corrupt even good characters.

jokes.text: Delicious motel furniture.
jokes.setup: Homer sets bait for beavers
jokes.punchline: He describes furniture as delicious
jokes.explanation: Homer's food-centric worldview applied to inappropriate situations.

jokes.text: That's a $17 deck of cards. - Let it go, Dad.
jokes.setup: Homer forgot to empty the minibar before using furniture as bait
jokes.punchline: He's upset about losing overpriced hotel items to beavers
jokes.explanation: Good Homer cheapskate moment - priorities focused on financial loss during adventure.

jokes.text: Finally, man has triumphed over a small furry animal.
jokes.setup: Homer successfully destroys the beaver dam
jokes.punchline: His grandiose declaration about defeating beavers
jokes.explanation: Homer's tendency to make epic proclamations about minor achievements.

jokes.text: Actually, it's Fresca. - Fresca?
jokes.setup: Native American offers ceremonial drink of bear urine
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's actually just Fresca soda
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Homer's disappointment about it being soda instead of bear urine is perfectly in-character.

jokes.text: Hell, no. They outlawed that in this state two years ago.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks if Clara is Luke's girlfriend when he calls her sister
jokes.punchline: Luke's casual reference to incest laws
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about rural stereotypes delivered with perfect casual timing.

jokes.text: Done something terrible. - Don't care. Just totally caught in the moment.
jokes.setup: Lisa tries to confess her misdirection of Clara
jokes.punchline: Luke dismisses her confession because he's romance-focused
jokes.explanation: Standard romantic comedy beat - confession attempt interrupted by romance.

jokes.text: But I'll be back in six months. - Yee-haw! - Blanks. They just don't feel the same.
jokes.setup: Sheriff announces he shot a Texas Ranger and is going to jail
jokes.punchline: He used blanks but misses the real feeling
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about fake Western violence - the sheriff's nostalgia for real bullets.

jokes.text: Who are you, the 'used to' police?
jokes.setup: Lisa complains about environmental changes using 'used to'
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissive made-up authority figure
jokes.explanation: Homer's creative dismissal of environmental concerns with made-up authority.

jokes.text: I'm one lesson short.
jokes.setup: Clara is drowning and can't swim
jokes.punchline: She's very specific about her swimming lesson deficit
jokes.explanation: Good specific detail in crisis situation - the precision makes it funny.

jokes.text: Well, that certainly justifies attempted murder.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains she misdirected Clara because of romantic jealousy
jokes.punchline: Clara's sarcastic response to Lisa's weak justification
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan reaction to Lisa's confession - perfectly calibrated sarcasm.

jokes.text: And when... Hello, handsome. - What's up, cootie breath?
jokes.setup: Lisa is giving advice about there being more important things than boys
jokes.punchline: Immediately gets distracted by attractive boy, Bart mocks her
jokes.explanation: Lisa immediately contradicts her own advice, with typical sibling mockery

jokes.text: Homer, those are elk. - I still hate them. Go back to Grosse Pointe.
jokes.setup: Homer insults what he thinks are rich tourists
jokes.punchline: Even after correction, he maintains his hatred of elk
jokes.explanation: Homer's stubborn refusal to back down from his mistake, plus the geographic specificity.

jokes.text: You're the kind of slicker my family left Central Park West to avoid.
jokes.setup: Luke learns Lisa deliberately misdirected his sister
jokes.punchline: His family moved from Manhattan to avoid city people like Lisa
jokes.explanation: Good character reveal - Luke's family background explains his moral standards.

jokes.text: You got a lot of chutzpah, missy.
jokes.setup: Luke continues criticizing Lisa
jokes.punchline: Cowboy uses Yiddish word
jokes.explanation: Cultural mixing - cowboy character's New York Jewish background showing through.

jokes.text: Who is he? - You, Homie. - Whoo-hoo! In your face, imaginary guy.
jokes.setup: Marge talks about finding a man you love so much it hurts
jokes.punchline: Homer celebrates victory over hypothetical rival
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his competitive instincts extend even to imaginary rivals. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Moe, Moe, Moe / How do you like me? How do you like me? / Moe, Moe, Moe / Why don't you like me? Nobody likes me
jokes.setup: Moe has his own song on the radio
jokes.punchline: His desperate, pathetic lyrics about wanting to be liked
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe characterization - his song reveals his desperate need for acceptance.

jokes.text: Oh, that can't be good for the struts.
jokes.setup: The family car is bouncing wildly from their ranch activities
jokes.punchline: Homer's mild concern about car maintenance during chaos
jokes.explanation: Homer's practical concerns during adventure - good understatement for chaotic situation.

tmdb_overview: When challenged to think up a new Christmas carol, Homer instead writes a song about how much he hates Ned Flanders. The song becomes such a hit that the Simpsons become sick of hearing it and take a trip to a dude ranch. Lisa develops affections for a local boy named Luke but becomes jealous when he hears him talking to a girl named Clara on the telephone. One night, she meets Clara and gives her the wrong directions, leading her to become lost. After Lisa discovers that Clara is actually Luke's sister, she rushes to find her. She saves Clara, and decides to tell Luke what happened, but he is offended and dumps her. Meanwhile, Bart and Homer decide to help a tribe of Native Americans who want a beaver dam removed so they can reclaim their land.
